--- title: Shakespeare's plays as networks date: 2026-05-31 author: - name: Michael C. Zeller url: https://michaelczeller.github.io orcid: 0000-0002-2422-3896 fig-cap-location: top description: "Shakespeare's plays described with network visualisations and other text analysis." image: shakespeare_photo.png twitter-card: image: "shakespeare_photo.png" open-graph: image: "shakespeare_photo.png" categories: - for students - Shakespeare --- William Shakespeare's plays are a continual source of fascination and delight. They also contain all sorts of features intriguing from digital humanities and data science perspectives. For a bit of fun and with very little explanation so far, below are visualisations of Shakespeare's plays, most especially as network graphs. ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 2 Play_Genre Mean_Scene_Length 1 Comedy 151. 2 History 136. 3 Romance 147. 4 Tragedy 140. ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Distribution of Scene Lengths in Shakespeare Plays. Plays ordered by mean scene length.](index_files/figure-html/fig-ridgelines-1.png){#fig-ridgelines width=672} ::: ::: The dataset contains **105,153** lines of speech that make up Shakespeare's plays. @fig-scatter1 shows spread of plays by their total word count and total number of speeches (as others have [shown](https://vizual-statistix.tumblr.com/post/45680711343/this-is-a-simple-scatter-plot-of-shakespeares-37)). ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-scatter1 .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Shakespeare plays by total words and number of speeches. ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Two-mode network of plays and their (named) characters. Naturally, the history plays share the most number of characters.](index_files/figure-html/fig-all-netz-1.png){#fig-all-netz width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-verboseness .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Most loquacious characters... . ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Shakespeare's Plays Summaries
Based on data from kaggle.com & Wikipedia
``` ::: ::: # A Comedy of Errors Set in the Greek city of Ephesus, The Comedy of Errors tells the story of two sets of identical twins who were accidentally separated at birth. Antipholus of Syracuse and his servant, Dromio of Syracuse, arrive in Ephesus, which turns out to be the home of their twin brothers, Antipholus of Ephesus and his servant, Dromio of Ephesus. When the Syracusans encounter the friends and families of their twins, a series of wild mishaps based on mistaken identities lead to wrongful beatings, a near-seduction, the arrest of Antipholus of Ephesus, and false accusations of infidelity, theft, madness, and demonic possession. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of A Comedy of Errors... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-acoe-overview-1.png){#fig-acoe-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-acoe-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` acoe bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-acoe-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-acoe-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-acoe-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-acoe-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-acoe-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-acoe-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # A Midsummer Night's Dream The play is set in Athens, and consists of several subplots that revolve around the marriage of Theseus and Hippolyta. One subplot involves a conflict among four Athenian lovers. Another follows a group of six amateur actors rehearsing the play which they are to perform before the wedding. Both groups find themselves in a forest inhabited by fairies who manipulate the humans and are engaged in their own domestic intrigue. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of A Midsummer nights dream... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-amnd-overview-1.png){#fig-amnd-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-amnd-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` amnd bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-amnd-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-amnd-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-amnd-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-amnd-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-amnd-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-amnd-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # A Winter's Tale King Leontes’ jealousy leads him to wrongly accuse his wife of infidelity, causing tragedy. Years later, redemption, reconciliation, and miraculous reunions restore hope and family bonds. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of A Winters Tale... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-awt-overview-1.png){#fig-awt-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-awt-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` awt bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-awt-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-awt-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-awt-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-awt-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-awt-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-awt-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # All's Well That Ends Well {{< video https://youtu.be/Z16KV7VxfIg?si=fTYJP5bZuqUv2pm6 start="1" >}} Helena cures the King of France’s illness and pursues her love, Bertram, through clever schemes. Challenges, misunderstandings, and social constraints are overcome, emphasizing perseverance and wit. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Alls well that ends well... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-awtew-overview-1.png){#fig-awtew-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-awtew-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` awtew bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-awtew-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-awtew-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-awtew-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-awtew-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-awtew-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-awtew-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Antony and Cleopatra The plot is based on Thomas North's 1579 English translation of Plutarch's Lives (in Ancient Greek) and follows the relationship between Cleopatra and Mark Antony from the time of the Sicilian revolt to Cleopatra's suicide during the War of Actium. The main antagonist is Octavius Caesar, one of Antony's fellow triumvirs of the Second Triumvirate and the first emperor of the Roman Empire. The tragedy is mainly set in the Roman Republic and Ptolemaic Egypt and is characterized by swift shifts in geographical location and linguistic register as it alternates between sensual, imaginative Alexandria and a more pragmatic, austere Rome. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Antony and Cleopatra... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-aac-overview-1.png){#fig-aac-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-aac-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` aac bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-aac-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-aac-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-aac-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-aac-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-aac-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-aac-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # As You Like It {{< video https://youtu.be/Jg1qTs5pdeM?si=XX_BXxx7UESMQx05 start="1" >}} As You Like It follows its heroine Rosalind as she flees persecution in her uncle's court, accompanied by her cousin Celia to find safety and, eventually, love, in the Forest of Arden. In the forest, they encounter a variety of memorable characters, notably the melancholy traveller Jaques, who speaks one of Shakespeare's most famous speeches ("All the world's a stage") and provides a sharp contrast to the other characters in the play, always observing and disputing the hardships of life in the country. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of As you like it... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-ayli-overview-1.png){#fig-ayli-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-ayli-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ayli bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-ayli-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-ayli-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-ayli-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-ayli-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-ayli-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-ayli-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Coriolanus Coriolanus is the name given to a Roman general after his military feats against the Volscians at Corioli. Following his success, others encourage Coriolanus to pursue the consulship, but his disdain for the plebeians and mutual hostility with the tribunes lead to his banishment from Rome. In exile, he presents himself to the Volscians, then leads them against Rome. After he relents and agrees to a peace with Rome, he is killed by his previous Volscian allies. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Coriolanus... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-corio-overview-1.png){#fig-corio-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-corio-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` corio bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-corio-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-corio-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-corio-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-corio-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-corio-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-corio-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Cymbeline Cymbeline, also known as The Tragedie of Cymbeline or Cymbeline, King of Britain, is a play by William Shakespeare set in Ancient Britain (c.10–14 AD) and based on legends that formed part of the Matter of Britain concerning the early historical Celtic British King Cunobeline. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Cymbeline... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-cymbe-overview-1.png){#fig-cymbe-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-cymbe-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` cymbe bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-cymbe-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-cymbe-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Hamlet {{< video https://youtu.be/muLAzfQDS3M?si=PpTHpdnBIqnVBJao start="1" >}} Set in Denmark, the play depicts Prince Hamlet and his attempts to exact revenge against his uncle, Claudius, who has murdered Hamlet's father in order to seize his throne and marry Hamlet's mother. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Hamlet... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hamlet-overview-1.png){#fig-hamlet-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hamlet-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hamlet bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hamlet-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hamlet-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-hamlet-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hamlet-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-hamlet-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-hamlet-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry IV It was composed in the later years of the reign of Elizabeth I, when questions of succession and political stability were prominent. Set in England in the early 1400s during the reign of Henry IV, the play depicts rebellion against the crown alongside the development of Prince Hal, the future Henry V, and examines themes of leadership and the formation of the heir apparent. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry IV... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hiv-overview-1.png){#fig-hiv-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hiv-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hiv bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hiv-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hiv-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-hiv-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hiv-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-hiv-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-hiv-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry V It tells the story of King Henry V of England, focusing on events immediately before and after the Battle of Agincourt (1415) during the Hundred Years' War. In the First Quarto text, it was titled The Cronicle History of Henry the fift and The Life of Henry the Fifth in the First Folio text. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry V... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hv-overview-1.png){#fig-hv-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hv-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hv bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hv-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hv-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-hv-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hv-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-hv-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-hv-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry VI Part 1 Henry VI, Part 1 deals with the loss of England's French territories and the political machinations leading up to the Wars of the Roses, as the English political system is torn apart by personal squabbles and petty jealousy. Henry VI, Part 2 deals with the King's inability to quell the bickering of his nobles and the inevitability of armed conflict and Henry VI, Part 3 deals with the horrors of that conflict. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry VI Part 1... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi1-overview-1.png){#fig-hvi1-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi1-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hvi1 bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi1-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi1-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-hvi1-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi1-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-hvi1-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-hvi1-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry VI Part 2 Henry VI, Part 2 (1591) is a Shakespearean history play about King Henry VI of England's inability to quell the bickering of his noblemen, the death of his trusted advisor Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester, and the political rise of Richard of York, 3rd Duke of York; it culminates with the First Battle of St Albans (1455), the initial battle of the Wars of the Roses, which were civil wars between the House of Lancaster and the House of York. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry VI Part 2... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi2-overview-1.png){#fig-hvi2-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi2-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hvi2 bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi2-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive network visualisation of characters and scenes in which they appear. ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network based on coappearance in scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi2-overview-coappearance-1.png){#fig-hvi2-overview-coappearance width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of scene coappearance and line totals"} ::: {.cell fig.widgth='10'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Network with edges/links based on coappearance in scenes and the number of lines spoken by the two characters in those scenes.](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi2-overview-lines-1.png){#fig-hvi2-overview-lines width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell fig.widgth='6'} ::: {#fig-hvi2-alluvial .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry VI Part 3 Whereas 1 Henry VI deals with the loss of England's French territories and the political machinations leading up to the Wars of the Roses and 2 Henry VI focuses on the King's inability to quell the bickering of his nobles, and the inevitability of armed conflict, 3 Henry VI deals primarily with the horrors of that conflict, with the once stable nation thrown into chaos and barbarism as families break down and moral codes are subverted in the pursuit of revenge and power. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry VI Part 3... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-hvi3-overview-1.png){#fig-hvi3-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi3-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` hvi3 bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-hvi3-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Henry VIII The Famous History of the Life of King Henry the Eighth, often shortened to Henry VIII, is a collaborative history play, written by William Shakespeare and John Fletcher, based on the life of Henry VIII. An alternative title, All Is True, is recorded in contemporary documents, with the title Henry VIII not appearing until the play's publication in the First Folio of 1623. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Henry VIII... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-h8-overview-1.png){#fig-h8-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-h8-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Julius Caesar {{< video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7X9C55TkUP8 start="29" >}} The play portrays the political conspiracy that led to the assassination of the Roman dictator Julius Caesar and Rome's subsequent civil war. Drawing primarily (with deviations in various aspects) from Sir Thomas North's 1579 translation of Parallel Lives by Plutarch, Shakespeare presents a dramatised account of Caesar's growing power, his murder by a group of senators led by Cassius and Brutus, and the defeat of the conspirators by the forces of Mark Antony and Octavius at the Battle of Philippi. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Julius Caesar... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-jc-overview-1.png){#fig-jc-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-jc-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # King John {{< video https://youtu.be/fpAZju8RbiI?si=Q30a1ERTZgS9NlC_ start="1" >}} The Life and Death of King John (also King John) is a history play about the reign of John, King of England (r. 1199–1216), the son of Henry II and Eleanor of Aquitaine, and the father of Henry III. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of King John... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-kj-overview-1.png){#fig-kj-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-kj-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # King Lear {{< video https://youtu.be/zn955417swY?si=OawMtSwX_dbO6YlH start="1" >}} Set in pre-Roman Britain, the play depicts the consequences of King Lear's love-test, in which he divides his power and land according to the praise of his daughters. The play is known for its dark tone, complex poetry, and prominent motifs concerning blindness, madness and human nature. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of King Lear... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-kl-overview-1.png){#fig-kl-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-kl-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Love's Labours Lost It follows the King of Navarre and his three companions as they attempt to swear off the company of women for three years in order to focus on study and fasting. Their subsequent infatuation with the Princess of France and her ladies makes them forsworn (break their oath). In an untraditional ending for a comedy, the play closes with the death of the Princess's father, and all weddings are delayed for a year. The play draws on themes of masculine love and desire, reckoning and rationalisation, and reality versus fantasy. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Loves Labours Lost... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-lll-overview-1.png){#fig-lll-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-lll-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Macbeth {{< video http://youtube.com/watch?v=zGbZCgHQ9m8 start="1" >}} In the play, a brave Scottish general named Macbeth receives a prophecy from a trio of witches that one day he will become King of Scotland. Consumed by his latent ambition and spurred to violence by his wife, Macbeth murders King Duncan and takes the Scottish throne for himself. Then, racked with guilt and paranoia, he commits further murders to protect himself from enmity and suspicion, becoming a tyrannical ruler in the process. The violence perpetrated by the power-hungry couple leads to their insanity and finally to their deaths. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of macbeth... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-macbeth-overview-1.png){#fig-macbeth-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-macbeth-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Measure for Measure The play centres on the despotic and puritan Angelo, a deputy entrusted to rule the city of Vienna in the absence of Duke Vincentio, who instead disguises himself as a humble friar to observe Angelos regency and the lives of his citizens. Angelo persecutes a young man, Claudio, for the crime of fornication, sentencing him to death on a technicality. Angelo then attempts to exploit Isabella (the sister of Claudio), a chaste and innocent nun, when she comes to plead for the life of her brother. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Measure for measure... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-mfm-overview-1.png){#fig-mfm-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-mfm-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # The Merchant of Venice A merchant in Venice named Antonio defaults on a large loan taken out on behalf of his dear friend, Bassanio, and provided by a Jewish moneylender, Shylock, with seemingly inevitable fatal consequences. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Merchant... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-merchant-overview-1.png){#fig-merchant-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-merchant-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Merchant bar... . ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-important collapse="true" title="Shylock: You call me misbeliever"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

You call me misbeliever (1.3.106–128)

SHYLOCK

Signior Antonio, many a time and oft

In the Rialto you have rated me

About my moneys and my usances:

Still have I borne it with a patient shrug,

For sufferance is the badge of all our tribe.

You call me misbeliever, cut-throat dog,

And spit upon my Jewish gaberdine,

And all for use of that which is mine own.

Well then, it now appears you need my help:

Go to, then, you come to me, and you say

'Shylock, we would have moneys:' you say so,

You, that did void your rheum upon my beard

And foot me as you spurn a stranger cur

Over your threshold: moneys is your suit

What should I say to you? Should I not say

'Hath a dog money? is it possible

A cur can lend three thousand ducats?' Or

Shall I bend low and in a bondman's key,

With bated breath and whispering humbleness, Say this,

'Fair sir, you spit on me on Wednesday last,

You spurn'd me such a day, another time

You call'd me dog, and for these courtesies

I'll lend you thus much moneys'?

``` ::: ::: {{< video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-vSR6W8_uBU start="1" >}} ::: ::: {.callout-important collapse="true" title="Portia: The quality of mercy"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

The quality of mercy (4.1.191–212)

PORTIA

The quality of mercy is not strain'd,

It droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven

Upon the place beneath: it is twice blest,

It blesseth him that gives and him that takes:

'Tis mightiest in the mightiest: it becomes

The throned monarch better than his crown,

His sceptre shows the force of temporal power,

The attribute to awe and majesty,

Wherein doth sit the dread and fear of kings,

But mercy is above this sceptred sway,

It is enthroned in the hearts of kings,

It is an attribute to God himself,

And earthly power doth then show likest God's

When mercy seasons justice. Therefore, Jew,

Though justice be thy plea, consider this,

That, in the course of justice, none of us

Should see salvation: we do pray for mercy,

And that same prayer doth teach us all to render

The deeds of mercy. I have spoke thus much

To mitigate the justice of thy plea,

Which if thou follow, this strict court of Venice

Must needs give sentence 'gainst the merchant there.

``` ::: ::: {{< video https://youtu.be/wmmBT_4dmI0?si=JOvB95hRUWeENT-z start="1" >}} ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Interactive two-mode network of players' scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-merchant-dynaNet .cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` Alluvial diagram of play acts and the number of lines by characters (only the top-10 characters in terms of total lines). ::: ::: # Merry Wives of Windsor It features the character Sir John Falstaff, the fat knight who had previously been featured in Henry IV, Part 1 and Part 2. Tradition has it that The Merry Wives of Windsor was written at the request of Queen Elizabeth I, who watching Henry IV, Part 1, is said to have asked Shakespeare to write a play depicting Falstaff in love. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Network of characters and scene appearances"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Overview of Merry Wives of Windsor... .](index_files/figure-html/fig-mww-overview-1.png){#fig-mww-overview width=672} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Top 15 characters by speaking lines"} ::: {.cell} ::: {#fig-mww-bar .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
